one_line_takeaway: A cross-paradigm literature survey (not a novel-method or empirical paper) that unifies differential privacy, federated learning, cryptographic PPML, and GAN-based synthetic-data methods under one IoT-deployment-focused taxonomy and maps privacy attacks (membership inference, model inversion, reconstruction, gradient leakage, traffic analysis) to their standard defences, while flagging fragmented evaluation practice and vision/energy-dataset concentration as open gaps.
key_finding: As a survey, the paper's 'findings' are synthesis conclusions rather than new empirical results: (1) no existing prior survey unifies all four PPML paradigms under one analytical framework grounded in concrete IoT deployment constraints (Table 2 benchmarks 9 prior surveys, each showing a specific gap -- e.g., Jiang et al. 2021 is DP-only and IIoT-specific, Chen et al. 2025 covers only cryptographic PPML and excludes DP/FL/IoT constraints, Raghav et al. 2025 covers only FL); (2) evaluation practice in the field is fragmented -- most surveyed works report only one of {formal DP guarantee (epsilon), empirical attack success (MIA AUC/ASR), or robustness}, rarely all three jointly; (3) benchmark datasets used across the PPML-IoT literature are concentrated in just two modalities -- vision (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, LFW, a Gender Identification face dataset) and electricity time-series (Pecan Street Dataport, the ECL/Electricity Consumption and Occupancy dataset, NREL) -- leaving mobility, audio, and industrial sensor data underrepresented as an open benchmarking gap; (4) of the surveyed open-source frameworks (TensorFlow Privacy, Opacus, Flower, PySyft, FATE), Flower combined with Opacus/TensorFlow Privacy is judged the most practical stack for IoT because of its framework-agnostic, heterogeneous-device-oriented FL support, while PySyft and FATE are judged poorly suited to constrained edge deployment.
core_contribution: This is a survey (not an empirical/methods paper). Its own contribution is a structured, IoT-centric cross-paradigm taxonomy unifying four PPML families -- perturbation-based methods (DP, DP-Bloom-Filter encoding), distributed learning (FL, FTL, SplitFed), cryptographic methods (HE, SMPC, TEE), and generative models (GAN variants) -- plus a systematic threat-to-defence mapping (membership inference, model inversion, reconstruction, gradient leakage, adversarial, traffic analysis attacks each tied to specific countermeasures), a consolidated comparison table of paradigm trade-offs (Table 3/4), a review of evaluation metrics/datasets/open-source frameworks (Tables 5/6), and a future-research roadmap for IoT deployment (energy-aware PPML, hybrid DP-cryptographic designs, privacy-aware foundation models, quantum-resilient PPML). The authors explicitly position their differentiator versus prior surveys (Table 2) as: (i) unifying paradigms usually studied in isolation, (ii) grounding discussion in concrete IoT deployment constraints (bandwidth, energy, edge execution, heterogeneity), and (iii) moving beyond descriptive summary to a threat-defence mapping and forward-looking roadmap. It reviews >200 publications from 2014-early 2026.
threat_model: Not a single threat model -- the paper catalogues the attack taxonomy used across the surveyed PPML literature, organized along two axes: adversary behaviour (passive/'honest-but-curious' observation of model behaviour vs. active interference such as injecting adversarial inputs or poisoned training data) and adversary knowledge (white-box access to model parameters/gradients vs. black-box access limited to model outputs/queries). Attack classes covered: membership inference (black-box output-probability based, and white-box parameter/gradient based), model inversion (reconstructing sensitive input attributes from prediction confidence/softmax outputs), reconstruction attacks (recovering raw inputs from stored intermediate/latent feature vectors), gradient leakage (reconstructing inputs from shared gradients in federated/distributed training, per Zhu et al.'s original gradient-leakage attack and its iDLG improvement), adversarial/evasion attacks (e.g., FGSM) framed as privacy-adjacent because they also intersect with training-data inference, and traffic analysis (inferring behaviour from encrypted communication metadata -- packet timing, payload size, update cadence -- especially in bandwidth-constrained LPWAN/FL settings).
limitations: As stated or implied by the paper itself: (1) it is a narrative/taxonomic survey with no new experiments, benchmarks, or quantitative comparisons of its own -- all quantitative figures (dataset sizes, framework capabilities) are drawn from the cited primary sources, not re-measured; (2) the authors themselves flag that benchmark coverage in the surveyed literature is concentrated on vision and electricity time-series data, with mobility traces, audio, and industrial sensor streams underrepresented (Section 7); (3) they note DP-BF-Encoding lacks standardized benchmarks against other PPML techniques and that DL-architecture compatibility remains limited (Section 9.1.3); (4) they note most surveyed works report privacy, utility, and robustness metrics separately rather than jointly (Section 6), which limits comparability across the very taxonomy the survey constructs; (5) the paper's own future-roadmap sections (quantum-aware privacy, foundation-model privacy) are speculative/forward-looking rather than validated.

datasets_used: MNIST (handwritten digits) -- cited as a low-complexity DP-utility baseline used across the surveyed literature, not the authors' own experiment
Fashion-MNIST -- 70,000 grayscale 28x28 images, 10 clothing classes, 60,000 train / 10,000 test, cited as widely used for benchmarking DP-SGD and MIA defences in the surveyed literature
CIFAR-10 -- 60,000 colour 32x32 images, 10 object classes, 50,000 train / 10,000 test, cited as the standard higher-complexity DP benchmark in the surveyed literature
Labeled Faces in the Wild (LFW) -- over 13,000 web-collected facial images, cited as used to evaluate model-inversion/reconstruction defences in the surveyed literature
Gender Identification dataset -- approx. 2,300 cropped facial images, cited as used for attribute-inference evaluation in the surveyed literature
Pecan Street Dataport -- circuit-level electricity consumption at 15-minute intervals through 2018, cited as used to evaluate DP against load-disaggregation/occupancy-inference attacks

Electricity Consumption and Occupancy (ECL) -- 15-minute consumption from 370 Portuguese clients (2011-2014), reduced to ~320 clients after preprocessing, cited as widely used for long-term forecasting/privacy-utility evaluation
National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) dataset -- 200 Midwestern US households sampled every 10 minutes (144 samples/household/day), cited as used to evaluate synthetic-data fidelity under DP
quantitative_results: This is a survey -- there are no experiments run or metrics measured by the authors themselves; all numeric figures listed above (dataset sizes/splits) are descriptive facts about datasets used by the cited primary-source papers, not results produced or reported by this survey's own evaluation.
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the empirical-baseline sense; the survey instead qualitatively benchmarks itself against 9 prior surveys in Table 2 (Amiri-Zarandi et al. 2020, Al-Garadi et al. 2020, Jiang et al. 2021, Abbas et al. 2022, Aouedi et al. 2024, Le et al. 2024, Wakili et al. 2025, Kaur et al. 2025, Chen et al. 2025, Demelius et al. 2025, Raghav et al. 2025), and qualitatively compares 5 open-source PPML frameworks (TensorFlow Privacy, Opacus, Flower/FLwr, PySyft, FATE) across DP support, FL support, privacy accounting, and IoT suitability (Table 6).
